key_insight: AI-generated synthetic AML control patients closely reproduced the results of a randomized trial, suggesting synthetic cohorts can stand in for real controls in some study designs.
clinical_relevance: This approach could reduce reliance on large control arms, potentially speeding oncology trials when patient accrual is difficult or diseases are highly stratified.
method: Fine-tuned a tabular foundation model on external multimodal AML data from 1,377 patients.
Generated synthetic patients that matched clinical and genetic features from prior trials and a real-world registry.
Matched synthetic controls to the SORAML intervention arm using Cox risk scores and compared trial outcomes.
evidence_signal: In the SORAML replication, the synthetic-control analysis produced a similar event-free survival result and treatment effect (HR 0.66, 95% CI 0.48-0.90) to the original trial (HR 0.64, 95% CI 0.47-0.87).
watch_item: Synthetic controls still need careful validation, because performance may depend on the quality, completeness, and representativeness of the external training data.
paper_title: In Silico Trial Simulation with Artificial Intelligence-Generated Synthetic Control Cohorts Reproduces Results of a Randomized Controlled Trial in Acute Myeloid Leukemia
